description: Extension onboarding UX in the side panel. When the side panel opens with 0 plugins, guide new users through setup with a welcome screen, checklist, and plugin install instructions. Distinguish first-time users (full onboarding) from returning users who removed all plugins (simpler empty state). Auto-transition to the plugin list when plugins are discovered. The side panel uses React 19 with RetroUI design system (Archivo Black headings, Space Grotesk body, yellow primary, flat shadows).

userStories.title: Design and implement welcome/onboarding state
userStories.description: As a new user, I want to see a welcome/onboarding view when the side panel opens for the first time with 0 plugins so that I understand what OpenTabs is and how to get started.
acceptanceCriteria: When side panel opens with 0 plugins and user has never had plugins before, the onboarding view is shown instead of the current EmptyState
Onboarding view shows 'Welcome to OpenTabs' heading in Archivo Black
Onboarding view shows 1-2 sentence explanation of what OpenTabs does (e.g., 'OpenTabs gives AI agents access to your web apps through your browser session')
Setup checklist is displayed with two items: 'MCP server running' (check mark if WebSocket connected) and 'Plugins installed' (check mark if plugin count > 0)
Checklist items use yellow accent for completed state and muted style for incomplete
Install instruction shown: example command npm install -g opentabs-plugin-slack
Search suggestion shown: run opentabs plugin search in terminal
All typography uses RetroUI design system (Archivo Black for headings, Space Grotesk for body)
Visual style uses flat shadows, yellow accents consistent with existing side panel components
bun run build passes
bun run type-check passes
bun run lint passes
bun run knip passes
bun run test passes
userStories.notes: Create a new OnboardingState component in platform/browser-extension/src/side-panel/components/. The existing EmptyState in EmptyStates.tsx handles the 0-plugin case — the new component replaces it for first-time users. The side panel already receives plugin count and connection status from the background script via message passing. Use these existing signals to drive the checklist state. The checklist should be a simple list with check/circle icons — not an interactive stepper. Keep the component self-contained. Reference the existing RetroUI patterns in the side panel (PluginCard, Header, etc.) for consistent styling. The onboarding view should feel welcoming but not overwhelming — prioritize clarity over decoration.

userStories.title: Transition from onboarding to normal state
userStories.description: As a user, I want the side panel to automatically transition from the onboarding view to the plugin list when plugins are discovered, and show the correct state on reconnection.
acceptanceCriteria: When plugins.changed event fires with plugin count > 0, the onboarding view transitions to the plugin list view
Transition is immediate (no animation delay needed, React re-render is sufficient)
When server disconnects, the disconnected state is shown (not the onboarding view)
When server reconnects and has plugins, the plugin list is shown
When server reconnects with 0 plugins AND user is a first-time user, the onboarding view is shown
When server reconnects with 0 plugins AND user previously had plugins, the returning-user empty state is shown (US-003)
No flickering between states during reconnection
bun run build passes
bun run type-check passes
bun run lint passes
bun run knip passes
bun run test passes
userStories.notes: The side panel App component (or its state management layer) already handles connected/disconnected states and plugin count. Add logic to determine which empty state to show: onboarding (first-time, 0 plugins) vs returning-user empty (previously had plugins, now 0) vs plugin list (has plugins). The connection state takes priority — if disconnected, always show disconnected state regardless of plugin count. The state transitions are driven by existing message handlers (plugins.changed, connection status changes). No new message types needed. Ensure the state machine is clear: disconnected > connected+plugins > connected+0plugins+first-time > connected+0plugins+returning.

userStories.title: Update empty state for returning users
userStories.description: As a returning user who has uninstalled all plugins, I want to see a simpler empty state (not full onboarding) so that I can quickly reinstall a plugin without re-reading the welcome content.
acceptanceCriteria: chrome.storage.local is used to persist a hasEverHadPlugins boolean flag
When plugin count transitions from 0 to >0 for the first time, hasEverHadPlugins is set to true
When hasEverHadPlugins is true AND current plugin count is 0, the returning-user empty state is shown
Returning-user empty state shows: 'No plugins installed' heading
Returning-user empty state shows install instruction: npm install -g opentabs-plugin-slack
Returning-user empty state does NOT show the full welcome/explanation text or setup checklist
The flag persists across extension restarts (chrome.storage.local survives service worker restarts)
bun run build passes
bun run type-check passes
bun run lint passes
bun run knip passes
bun run test passes
userStories.notes: Use chrome.storage.local.get/set to persist hasEverHadPlugins. Read the flag on side panel mount (it's async, so start with a loading/null state and resolve quickly). When plugins.changed fires with count > 0, set the flag to true. The existing EmptyState component in EmptyStates.tsx can be updated to serve as the returning-user empty state — it may already be close to what's needed. The key distinction is: first-time users see the full onboarding (US-001), returning users see a minimal 'no plugins, here's how to install one' message. The chrome.storage.local API is already available in the side panel context (no new permissions needed).

userStories.title: Add E2E tests for onboarding states
userStories.description: As a platform developer, I want E2E tests covering the onboarding state transitions so that regressions are caught automatically.
acceptanceCriteria: Test: fresh extension with 0 plugins shows the onboarding view with welcome heading and setup checklist
Test: adding a plugin (server discovers it) transitions the side panel from onboarding to plugin list
Test: disconnected state shows correctly when server stops (not onboarding)
Tests run in the existing Playwright E2E framework
Tests use the existing fixtures (MCP server, extension, test server) from e2e/fixtures.ts
bun run test:e2e passes
userStories.notes: Add a new E2E test file e2e/onboarding.e2e.ts. Use the existing test fixtures which provide an MCP server, loaded extension, and controllable test server. For the 0-plugins test: start the server with an empty localPlugins config, open the side panel, assert the onboarding heading and checklist are visible. For the transition test: start with 0 plugins, open side panel, verify onboarding, then add a plugin to config and trigger reload, verify the side panel transitions to show the plugin card. For the disconnected test: start server, open side panel, stop the server, verify the disconnected state is shown. The side panel page can be accessed via chrome-extension://<id>/side-panel/index.html in Playwright. Reference existing E2E tests (tool-dispatch.e2e.ts, lifecycle.e2e.ts) for patterns.

userStories.title: Update CLAUDE.md for onboarding UX
userStories.description: As a platform developer, I want CLAUDE.md to document the onboarding state machine and the hasEverHadPlugins persistence so that future changes maintain the correct behavior.
acceptanceCriteria: CLAUDE.md Chrome Extension section mentions the side panel onboarding flow
The three side panel states for 0-plugin scenarios are documented: onboarding (first-time), returning-user empty, disconnected
The hasEverHadPlugins chrome.storage.local flag is mentioned
Documentation is high-level (architecture/patterns, not implementation details) per CLAUDE.md guidelines
No individual plugin names are listed per CLAUDE.md guidelines
userStories.notes: Edit CLAUDE.md. Add a brief note to the Chrome Extension section or Architecture section about the side panel state machine for empty states. Keep it concise — 2-3 sentences max. Mention the three states (onboarding, returning-user empty, disconnected) and that chrome.storage.local persists the hasEverHadPlugins flag. Do not go into component-level detail — the code is the source of truth for implementation.
